title: The Unlucky Ones: What It Feels Like to Get Struck By Lightning
gist: Profiles of lightning-strike survivors — Roy Sullivan struck seven times, and others like Mary Anning and surgeon Tony Cicoria who developed unusual talents afterward — illustrate that strikes more often deliver lasting agony than 'genius.'

insights.claim: Former Virginia park ranger Roy Sullivan survived seven direct lightning strikes between 1942 and 1977, sustaining repeated burns and nerve damage, documented in ranger logs and medical records.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: Vivid human-interest science; no market read.
insights.quote: survived seven direct lightning strikes between 1942 and 1977
insights.locator: The former Virginia park ranger and fire lookout

insights.claim: Surgeon Tony Cicoria developed a sudden passion for piano and composing after a 1994 lightning strike, including a piece dubbed The Lightning Sonata, a case recounted by neurologist Oliver Sacks in Musicophilia.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: Illustrates rare neurological after-effects of electrical trauma.
insights.quote: took a sudden interest in piano and began composing music after he was struck by lightning in 1994
insights.locator: Tony Cicoria, a semi-retired orthopedic surgeon
